planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil temperatures are consistently above 65°F; rake seed lightly into the top quarter inch of soil and keep the seedbed moist until germination, then reduce watering as the sward fills in.

directions: Broadcast seed at 5–10 lb per 1,000 sq ft after the last frost once soil is reliably warm, lightly raking it into the top quarter inch and rolling the seedbed to ensure good seed-to-soil contact. Bahia grass is the dominant turf and pasture grass of Florida and the Gulf Coast, prized for its exceptional drought tolerance and ability to thrive in the poor, sandy, acidic soils where other grasses fail. Once established, deep rhizomes and a dense root system allow it to survive extended dry periods with little or no supplemental irrigation, making it highly cost-effective for large lawns, roadsides, and pastures. Mow regularly to 3–4 inches to suppress the tall, forked seed stalks that emerge quickly in summer; no fertilizer is required on most native Gulf Coast soils.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring onto a lightly disturbed, open soil surface in full sun; press seed into contact with the soil without deep burial and keep the seedbed evenly moist until germination occurs in two to three weeks.

directions: Scatter seed onto a lightly raked seedbed in full sun in early spring, pressing it firmly into the soil and watering until seedlings emerge and begin to establish their root systems over the first month. Pitchfork paspalum is a native warm-season grass of the southeastern coastal plain and eastern prairies, recognized by its distinctive forked seed heads — two to several spreading racemes that give the species its name — which emerge in mid to late summer and persist attractively into fall. It thrives in open, well-drained prairies, pine savannas, and dry roadsides, tolerating drought and lean soils once the deep root system is established, and providing important seed and cover for grassland birds. Allow plants to self-sow into adjacent open ground to gradually build a naturalized stand; division of established clumps in spring is also effective for expanding plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after the last frost onto bare, rocky, or sandy soil in full sun; press seed lightly into the surface and water once to settle it, then rely on natural rainfall as seedlings are drought-tolerant from an early age.

directions: Broadcast seed onto bare, well-drained rocky or gravelly soil in full sun at the last frost date, pressing it lightly into the surface and watering once to ensure contact; subsequent irrigation is rarely needed as seedlings establish quickly in warm conditions. Green sprangletop is a native of rocky hillsides, dry plains, and open scrub from the southwestern US through Mexico and into the tropics, where its loose, arching seed heads with multiple side branches add airy, open texture to dry-landscape plantings and wildlife gardens. Established plants are highly drought-tolerant and thrive in the lean, alkaline, or shallow soils that challenge most other grasses, making this species an excellent choice for erosion control on dry slopes and rocky outcroppings. The abundant seed production supports finches, sparrows, and other seed-eating birds through fall and into winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring at the base of a trellis, shrub, or tree that will support the vine's twining growth; sow seeds 1 inch deep in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and keep the area consistently moist until germination.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in spring at the base of a fence, trellis, or woody shrub in partial shade with moist, humus-rich soil, providing a support structure immediately so the twining stems can climb as they emerge. Anglepod milkvine is a native perennial of southeastern US woodlands, thicket edges, and streamside clearings, producing star-shaped, greenish-white flowers in the milkweed family that are followed by distinctively angled, warty seed pods splitting to release silky-plumed seeds in late summer. As a member of the milkweed family, it serves as a larval host plant for monarch butterflies and queen butterflies alongside true milkweeds, making it a valuable addition to wildlife gardens in shaded or woodland-edge sites where common milkweed does not thrive. The vine dies back to a perennial rootstock each winter and re-emerges reliably in spring; avoid disturbing the root zone once plants are established.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings in spring into moist, fertile soil at the base of a sturdy fence, arbor, or large tree; plant in a location that receives at least a few hours of direct sun to encourage fruiting, and water well through the first growing season.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into moist, organically rich soil at the base of a large, sturdy support structure, watering thoroughly and mulching to retain moisture through the first growing season while the root system establishes. Heartleaf peppervine is a vigorous deciduous vine native to floodplain forests and bottomland thickets of the southeastern US, recognized by its broad, bold, heart-shaped leaves and the showy clusters of small berries that ripen from green through turquoise to blue-black in fall, providing an important food source for mockingbirds, robins, cedar waxwings, and other songbirds. This large-scale native vine is well suited to covering fences, large arbors, and the trunks of substantial trees in moist, lowland sites where vigorous growth is desired and space is not limiting. Once established on fertile, consistently moist soil, it requires no supplemental care and will cover its support structure rapidly; direct new growth as needed to keep the vine on its intended structure.
